item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh. I just got the punch at the end of the story. It thinks that it's Shakespeare, but it can't read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely agree. It doesn't really interest me, it just seems like a shakespeare clone that is sad for some reason. Maybe I'm just not getting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit too emotionally manipulative. It manages to tug at my heartstrings, but less so due to the story that's being told, and moreso due to a few cheap storytelling tricks. It honestly leaves a bad taste in my mouth. I'm novoting for the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this one.

I do think there are some modifications that can be made to make it fit in more with the site. For instance, I think the article would be more effective if it wasn't explicitly stated that we are looking at an imperfect Shakespeare clone. Perhaps his name can be blotted out for that effect. Maybe there can even be some ambiguity as to whether or not this is an actual genetic clone of the playwright. After all, there are circles of scholarship that debate his identity and his existence to this very day: even attributing his plays to other figures. It makes me wonder how they were able to get his genetic material and how they knew if it was right.

Perhaps that would make SCP-3564's fate even more tragic: in that not only did they keep making degenerated clones of him, but even the words that he so desperately feels a connection with and wants to communicate but can't due to his developmental issues aren't even his words, but those that his creators and handlers attempted to engender within him.

This way, as well, there can be ambiguity as to whether or not the failed clones were of Shakespeare or other English playwrights, like Marlowe: whom you do mention as a protocol.

But I really like the idea and the sympathetic nature of this one. The line about the failed clone without skin was also quite creepy and masterfully done. And I do agree with Roget that I would love to see records of his creators attempting to use him in a recital. And I so expecting there to be a CALIBAN reference. Or even a "If these shadows have offended" adapted into something like a protocol, or procedure. But good work. I hope you will continue developing this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really see what everyone else seems to here, the main description didn't give me much reason to feel invested other than it being supposedly Shakespeare. The interviewer dialogue felt very wooden, like something out of a middle school stage play. I would have preferred to see some examples of the recitals, would give more character than an interview where one party sounds inhuman and the other can hardly communicate but through bracketed speech. There's some promise in the concept but this execution doesn't really do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good friend for Jesus' sake forbear,

To dig the dust enclosed here.

Blessed be the man that spares these stones,

And cursed be he that moves my bones.

Poor Bill was doomed from the start. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fuck did they do to Shakespeare? D: Oh god, I am so [SAD] after that interview.

Remember, he's not Shakespeare, but Shakespeare's Monster

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel the need to give Bill a hug now.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
you did such a good goddamn job with this. big bill's character comes through sweet and clear like church bells, and he can't even talk. i'd be very interested in some kind of continuation of his story, as i may or may not have completely fallen in love with the gigantic unfortunate goober.

+1, +1, and +1 again until the stars grow cold

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's nothing wrong with Bill…he's just a-head of the curve! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay! It's up! Without a doubt the stupidest thing I've ever posted, but I'll be damned if it wasn't fun to write. I love this good mutant boi.

My everlasting gratitude goes out to the numerous people who critiqued this in chat, with a special thanks to CadaverCommander, who put up with my endless whining and gave me some great characterisation advice. They also do some incredibly good writing, which I strongly urge you to read.

Image attribution: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Shippingport_LOC_135430pu.jpg It's in the public domain, and I cropped it down and altered the colouring. Hopefully that's not against any laws or anything.


© Forsooth Productions, 1616. All rights hath been reserved. No William Shakespeares were harmed during the making of this scip.
